[10:14 AM] John (Driver): Hey dispatch, my scanner is freezing again when I try to scan oversized items at the drop-off point. Giving me "Error 404-X".
[10:16 AM] Sarah (Dispatch): Hey John, IT knows about this. Until the patch tomorrow, don't use the quick-scan button for oversized.
[10:17 AM] Sarah (Dispatch): You need to manually select "Oversized Load" from the dropdown menu first, THEN scan the barcode. If it still freezes, force close the app, clear the cache in Android settings, and log back in.
[10:18 AM] John (Driver): Ah okay, selecting from dropdown first worked. Thanks!

[11:02] Raj (Sorting Line B): Guys, belt 4 has a leaking box. Smells like bleach or cleaning chemicals. 
[11:03] Sarah (Ops Manager): Did someone hit the E-Stop yet?! 
[11:03] Raj (Sorting Line B): Yes, belt is stopped. But it's dripping on the floor. What's the protocol for chemical liquids? Do I just mop it?
[11:05] Sarah (Ops Manager): NO. Do not touch it with a normal mop. 
[11:06] Sarah (Ops Manager): 1. Keep everyone at least 3 meters away. 
[11:06] Sarah (Ops Manager): 2. Go to Pillar G and grab the Yellow Spill Kit. 
[11:07] Sarah (Ops Manager): 3. You MUST put on the nitrile gloves and goggles from the kit before doing anything else. 
[11:08] Sarah (Ops Manager): 4. Pour the absorbent granules over the spill, wait exactly 5 minutes for it to clump, then sweep it into the red hazmat bin. 
[11:09] Sarah (Ops Manager): Once it's cleaned, call EHS on speed dial 3 to report it.
[11:10] Raj (Sorting Line B): Copy that. Getting the kit now.

Customer Credit Approval and New Customer Onboarding Process

Document Status: Draft (Unstandardized)Business Unit: DHL Logistics OperationsProcess Owner: Operations & FinanceLast Updated: Not maintained consistently



1. Purpose

The purpose of this Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) is to document the end-to-end process for new customer onboarding with credit approval in DHL Logistics Operations. This SOP aims to explain how new customers are set up in operational and financial systems, how credit checks are conducted, and how approvals or rejections are handled.

Currently, this process is handled through a mix of emails, spreadsheets, informal instructions, and experience-based knowledge. This document consolidates existing practices but has not been fully standardized. The intention is to later refine this SOP into a clean and official knowledge article.



2. Scope

This SOP applies to:

Operations teams

Customer Service teams

Finance and Credit Control teams

Supervisors and Managers involved in customer onboarding

This SOP covers:

New customer request intake

Credit evaluation and approval

System setup

Communication with stakeholders

It does not cover customer contract negotiation or legal agreement signing.



3. Definitions and Abbreviations

CRM – Customer Relationship Management system

SAP – Enterprise Resource Planning system used for billing and finance

Credit Limit – Maximum allowed outstanding amount for a customer

Cash Customer – Customer required to prepay before shipment

Credit Customer – Customer allowed to ship and pay later based on approved credit terms



4. Roles and Responsibilities

4.1 Sales Team

Submit new customer onboarding request

Provide complete customer information

Support clarifications during credit review

4.2 Operations Team

Validate onboarding request completeness

Coordinate with Finance for system setup

Ensure customer is ready for operational use

4.3 Finance / Credit Control Team

Perform credit checks

Approve or reject credit requests

Set credit terms and limits in SAP

4.4 Management

Approve exceptional cases

Resolve escalations and delays



5. Process Overview

The new customer onboarding and credit approval process consists of the following high-level steps:

New customer request received

Initial validation of request

Credit evaluation

Approval or rejection decision

System setup

Confirmation to stakeholders

Delays frequently occur due to incomplete information, unclear ownership, or delayed approvals.



6. Detailed Process Steps



6.1 New Customer Request Submission

New customer onboarding requests are typically initiated by the Sales team. Requests are submitted through email or shared templates. There is no single standardized form currently used across all regions.

Minimum information required includes:

Legal company name

Company registration number

Billing address

Operational address (if different)

Estimated monthly volume

Requested credit limit

Requested payment terms (e.g., 30 days)

Some requests arrive with missing or inconsistent information, which causes delays.



6.2 Initial Validation by Operations

Upon receiving the request, Operations performs a basic validation to ensure that all required information is provided. This step is usually done manually by checking emails and attachments.

Common issues found during this stage:

Missing registration documents

Incomplete billing address

No estimated shipment volume provided

Unclear payment terms

When information is missing, Operations contacts Sales for clarification. This back-and-forth often happens over multiple emails.



6.3 Credit Evaluation by Finance

Once the request is validated, it is forwarded to the Finance or Credit Control team for evaluation. The credit evaluation process may include:

Internal credit history check (if existing customer in other countries)

External credit bureau checks

Assessment of requested credit limit versus shipment volume

Review of financial documents

Based on the evaluation, Finance decides whether the customer should be:

Approved as a credit customer

Approved with a lower credit limit

Approved as cash-only

Rejected

This decision-making process is not always documented clearly.



6.4 Approval Scenarios

6.4.1 Credit Approved as Requested

If the credit request is approved:

Finance confirms the approved credit limit and payment terms

Details are shared with Operations

System setup can begin

6.4.2 Credit Approved with Conditions

Sometimes credit is approved with conditions such as:

Reduced credit limit

Shorter payment terms

Partial prepaid requirement

These conditions are communicated via email but not always captured in a structured format.

6.4.3 Credit Rejected

If credit is rejected:

Customer may be offered cash-only terms

Sales is informed to manage customer expectations

Request may be closed or reworked



6.5 System Setup

After credit approval, the Operations and Finance teams proceed with system setup.

Typical system activities include:

Creating customer record in CRM

Creating customer master data in SAP

Assigning billing and operational roles

Configuring tax and invoicing parameters

System setup errors are common due to manual data entry.



6.6 Common System Setup Issues

Frequently observed issues include:

Duplicate customer records

Incorrect billing currency

Wrong tax code assigned

Credit limit mismatch between email approval and SAP setup

When issues occur, tickets are raised to IT or Finance, causing delays.



6.7 Confirmation to Stakeholders

Once setup is completed:

Operations confirms readiness for shipment processing

Sales is informed that the customer is live

Finance monitors the first few invoices closely

There is no standardized checklist to confirm all steps are completed before go-live.



7. Exception Handling and Escalations

7.1 Delayed Credit Approval

If credit approval takes more than 5 working days:

Operations escalates to Finance Manager

Sales may request temporary cash terms to start operations



7.2 Urgent Customer Go-Live Requests

In some cases, urgent onboarding is required. Temporary workarounds include:

Cash-only setup

Manual billing

Later credit conversion

These practices increase operational risk.



8. Risks and Known Gaps

The following issues have been identified:

Heavy manual processing

Lack of standardized forms

No centralized tracking

Dependency on email communication

Limited audit trail

New joiners often struggle to understand the process due to lack of clear documentation.



9. Compliance and Audit Considerations

Improper customer setup can lead to:

Revenue leakage

Bad debt exposure

Compliance violations

Audit findings

Finance periodically reviews customer credit exposure, but corrections are reactive rather than preventive.



10. Training and Knowledge Sharing

Currently, training is conducted informally through:

Shadowing experienced staff

Email instructions

Chat-based guidance

This SOP is intended to evolve into a proper training reference.



11. Summary

This SOP documents the current-state process for customer credit approval and onboarding. While it captures real operational practices, it lacks standardization, structure, and automation. The content should be transformed into a cleaner SOP, simplified checklist, and searchable Knowledge Base articles.

General Background

In DHL Logistics Operations, shipment exceptions happen daily due to multiple reasons such as missing information, system validation failures, incorrect routing, or customer-provided errors. These exceptions are usually picked up by the operations team either through system alerts, emails, or messages from supervisors. Because different teams handle exceptions differently, the steps documented here are inconsistent and sometimes incomplete.

Most of the time, agents rely on chat messages, emails, or screenshots instead of a standardized SOP. This leads to delays, repeated mistakes, and inconsistent customer communication. The notes below come from MS Teams chats, emails, informal training notes, and system error screenshots.





Common Shipment Exception Types Observed

Based on recent discussions and incidents, the most common shipment exception types include:

Invalid routing code

Missing or incorrect customer address

Incorrect country or postcode mapping

Weight or dimension mismatch

Duplicate shipment reference

System authorization errors

POD upload failures

Different resolutions were applied by different agents, often without proper documentation.









Informal Step-by-Step Notes 

When shipment exception appears, first check the exact error message.

Do not immediately escalate to IT without basic checks.

Verify customer address (postcode, city, country).

Confirm weight and dimensions are realistic.

Refresh booking screen and retry.

If still failing, take screenshot and escalate.

No official timeline or escalation matrix was included.



Known Gaps and Issues Identified

No single standardized SOP exists for shipment exceptions.

Resolution steps vary by agent and team.

Critical steps are hidden in chat messages and emails.

New joiners rely heavily on experienced staff instead of documentation.

Repeated errors occur because learnings are not captured formally.
